called the Bizarro Planet, where, and there's a bizarre version of Superman, and everything is the exact opposite of what it is in the normal real world, right? So everything that's right here is wrong there, and everything that's wrong, you know, is right. So for example, we have speed limits. They have, if you don't drive as fast as you possibly can, they pull you over and give you a ticket. So you've got to be going as fast as the car will go, or that's a violation of the law here. And so this is a little bit about that. It's about what they are being disrespected for and not accepted for and not celebrated in their outside world life is exactly what we glorify and validate and applaud and recognize has correct over here in our place. And so naturally, they want to be in our place more than they want to be in any other place. And so there's the general sense of I want to be a part of something. And even, you know, behavior belies talk. So even people who claim to be loners belong to something. If nothing else, they belong to the idea of the virtue of being a loner. And they associate with other loners. So in a general sense, the belonging thing is important. In a specific sense, the belonging to a place where they're right and where their virtues are accepted and encouraged is extremely powerful. There's some heftier stuff again. There's a couple of pages that are, I think, telling. The line is, and so one man created two houses and all men would forever want to go to those houses to be inside. And so hefter created both the houses as metaphor for lifestyle as well as an actual physical place that forevermore men of at least two generations desperately wanted to get inside that house. Oh boy, I'd like to see what's in that house. I'd like to be in that house for just a day. I'd like to live like that. Wouldn't it be cool? And so that's what we're about, is creating an actual place as well as a metaphor that our people want to be in and want to get into and want to stay into as much as they possibly can on page 96B, as I told you, cheap unsupervised employee. There's a little box at the top, the existential importance of Heff's house explained. As a Playboy cartoon in 1970, a man is clambered to the mountain peak to beg wisdom from a cross-legged guru. The guru tells the man, in a place called Chicago, there's a man who lives in a mansion full of beautiful women and wears pajamas all the time. Sit at his feet and learn from him, for he has found the secret of true happiness. So what's the existential importance of the place you present to your audience that verifies, that evidences to them that you have found the secret of true success, true happiness, true fulfillment? And if you got a great answer to that question, you have everybody trying to get into your place. Most of the cult activity is the same activity used in human potential movements and used in MLM marketing when it's done, effectively and it ought not to be ignored by us. I gave you a few pages from a very good book on this subject, starting on page 97. And what you should know is, the book isn't even available, at least to my knowledge it's not available. But what you should keep in mind is that writing can substitute for manual labor. So most cult recruiting is done through manual labor. Most MLM recruiting is still done through manual labor. But we can substitute the written word for the manual labor and have the same effect. So cults have closed borders. You're either in or you're not. So it's very important that we have closed borders, that there's a sense of you are either in or you're not. Back to your thing about your boot camp, right? Sure, to your members, you're absolutely right. That needs to be positioned as a members only event and by God you can't, now if you're going to go get non-members in, the marketing better not cross each other because you've got to tell them a very different story. Members only clubs always have guest passes. One doesn't get in the way of the other, it's two entirely different stories. So there's a little stage thing here at the bottom of that page. Stage one, the curious consumer crosses the members only firewall. So we haven't walked through a door. It's a very clear, visible, understandable door. You're over here, you come through this door, you wind up over here, here's what this place looks like. The hand-picked consumer gets, they're using the term brand washed instead of brain washed. Now what's real important in that line is the hand-picked because this is a lot about source and we're going to talk about that later. Stage three, the dedicated brand tribe member helps create a parallel social universe. So the members are now interacting with each other, encouraging each other, supporting each other, involved with each other. Groups exchange, here's something, groups encourage isolation from family and friends, free personal sacrifices, dictate a regimented lifestyle, and are guided by a single strong leader. So okay, we don't actually take them away from their family and friends, but what we do need to do is make sure that we counter the negative influences of the family and friends. So translate to my work. If you pay attention, there's a fair amount of repetition within what I do, talking to them about their employees, their family and their friends who don't get our kind of marketing, and we'll try and talk them out of it, and we'll make fun of them. And so I'm trying to separate them from those people all the way along. I have a story I use often, so you've probably heard it, about the client who owns, it happens to be, you guys are in a furniture business, the story is about a client that owns high end furniture stores who every year spends $50,000 printing up magnificent full color brochures designed by an ad agency in order to give 100 or so of them to his wife and burn the rest that they serve no purpose, but in order to have peace at home, he has to give her a marketing piece that she can take to the country club and show her friends that doesn't embarrass her like all of the marketing he does that actually makes sense. I tell that story as the separation device to alert you to the fact that most of the people around you in your world are going to hate everything we're telling you to do and are going to pick on you and are going to attack you and are going to try and get you not to do that and you have to intellectually and emotionally separate yourself from all that. You have to pick a side. Them or us.
